Union Defence Minister Rajnath Singh on Tuesday held a ministerial-level bilateral meeting with Vietnamese Minister of National Defence Phan Van Giang in Hanoi, as India and Vietnam reviewed expanding defence ties and regional security cooperation.

During the talks, Singh highlighted the historical foundations of the India-Vietnam relationship and underscored the significance of his visit coinciding with the 136th birth anniversary of former Vietnamese President Ho Chi Minh.

The Defence Minister also emphasised that the visit comes at a key moment in bilateral relations, marking ten years of the Comprehensive Strategic Partnership between the two countries.
@SpokespersonMoD

tweets.title:
The U.S. Department of State has made a determination approving a possible Foreign Military Sale to the Government of India for its purchase of Apache Helicopters follow-on support services and related equipment. The estimated total cost is $198.2 million.

The Government of India has requested to buy AH-64E Apache sustainment support services; U.S. Government and contractor engineering, technical, and logistics support services; technical data and publications; personnel training; and other related elements of logistics and program support.  

This proposed sale will support the foreign policy and national security objectives of the United States by helping to strengthen the U.S.-Indian strategic relationship and to improve the security of a major defense partner which continues to be an important force for political stability, peace, and economic progress in the Indo-Pacific and South Asia regions.

The proposed sale will improve India’s capability to meet current and future threats, strengthen its homeland defense, and deter regional threats.  India will have no difficulty absorbing these articles and services into its armed forces.
tweets.content:

In a remarkable turn of events that marks one of the most dramatic comebacks in modern Indian military history, Brigadier Shrikant Prasad Purohit has assumed charge as Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at Headquarters South Western Command, Jaipur.

Once at the center of the 2008 Malegaon blast case, Purohit spent nearly nine years in jail and fought a legal battle that lasted close to 18 years. Throughout the ordeal, he maintained that his interactions with extremist elements were part of authorized Military Intelligence operations aimed at infiltrating and monitoring suspicious networks.

In July 2025, the NIA Special Court acquitted him and all other accused, concluding that the prosecution failed to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt. Following his acquittal, the Indian Army restored his career progression, promoted him to Brigadier, and entrusted him with one of the most sensitive intelligence appointments in the force.

As Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at South Western Command, headquartered in Jaipur, he will oversee strategic intelligence assessments related to the western theatre, including Rajasthan’s key border districts such as Bikaner, Sri Ganganagar, Jaisalmer, Barmer, and adjoining sectors linked to Punjab and Gujarat. His role will involve monitoring Pakistani military deployments, drone intrusions, cross-border smuggling routes, terror networks, and emerging hybrid warfare threats.

For many in the defence community, this appointment symbolizes institutional confidence, professional vindication, and the extraordinary resilience of an officer who returned from one of the most controversial legal battles in India to occupy a coveted Military Intelligence position at the heart of the nation’s western security architecture.
